# Frontend Standards

## Summary

Across multiple repos, the shared frontend bar is intentional design, explicit taste translation, accessibility, and rendered verification. Product-specific design systems still belong in each repo.

When a maintained frontend overlay already matches the requested direction, use it before inventing a fresh style variant from scratch.

For professional product websites with no stronger repo-local direction, use [[Frontend Overlay - Polished Product Website]] as the default overlay.

## Shared Expectations

- do not ship generic template UI by default
- start from repo-local design docs, existing product UI, or an explicit taste brief before writing large UI blocks
- match typography and layout to the task: productive for dense task work, expressive for scanning and editorial moments
- keep spacing, sizing, and alignment on a constrained rhythm instead of arbitrary one-off values
- translate the chosen style into primitives before scaling implementation: semantic color roles, typography ladders, surface levels, control families, and section shells
- reserve expressive motion for significant moments; keep default motion subtle, fast, and out of the way
- use a real motion system when the interaction depends on sequencing, scroll choreography, or timeline control
- preserve accessibility basics, especially strong focus states and reduced-motion support
- verify responsive behavior deliberately
- run [[Frontend Quality Gate]] before calling substantial visual work done
- prefer token-driven systems over ad hoc styling
- avoid raw hex, radius, and timing scatter inside component code when the same role repeats more than once
- prefer maintained overlays and source packs for recurring taste profiles instead of re-deriving the same style repeatedly
- run the relevant lint, typecheck, build, and browser checks
- default to one dominant base and one accent for polished product sites unless the user or repo requires a richer palette
- avoid decorative gradients, blobs, bokeh, glow, and excessive glass unless the selected source system specifically calls for them
- keep shadows soft, rare, and hierarchy-driven; do not use heavy elevation to make weak structure feel premium

## Primitive Stack

Treat durable frontend systems as a layered stack instead of a pile of isolated classes.

- semantic tokens first: canvas, surface, border, text, action, focus, motion, and spacing roles
- typography ladders next: named display and body roles rather than one-off font sizes
- page and section shells after that: repeated container widths, vertical rhythm, grid counts, and inset rules
- component primitives next: control heights, radii, border treatments, and state treatments
- utilities or component-local classes last: implementation convenience should express the system, not replace it

If a live reference site is the source, inspect its computed styles and CSS structure when feasible so the primitive stack is grounded in something more precise than screenshots alone.

## Motion Guidance

- prefer CSS for small transitions and minor state changes
- prefer GSAP for choreographed motion, scroll-linked storytelling, pinned sequences, or interactions that need explicit timeline control
- do not improvise large animation systems from scattered delays and one-off transforms
- treat motion like part of the product surface, not decoration layered on at the end

## What Belongs Here

- reusable quality bar
- routing to design and polish skills
- reminders to inspect repo-local design docs first
- durable frontend overlays that encode recurring taste systems without replacing repo-local branding
- taste-translation guidance
- anti-pattern recognition
- review criteria for visual quality
- a cross-project boundary for when GSAP is justified

## What Stays Local

- product-specific tokens
- brand voice
- exact motion system
- component inventory
- marketing layout strategy

## Best Skill Pairings

- see [[Skill Routing Catalog]] for the primary frontend chooser
- `reference-to-system`: preferred when the system must be extracted before coding
- `premium-reference-frontend`: use when a concrete reference exists and implementation is expected
- `premium-frontend-build`: default implementation skill once the direction is already clear
- `redesign-existing-projects`: use when upgrading an existing site or app without rewriting it
- `ui-polish-pass`: use after implementation for a focused quality pass
- [[Frontend Quality Gate]]: final visual evidence gate for substantial frontend work
